video_title: Fission Impossible? Financing New Nuclear w/ Michael Seely
gist: Nuclear analyst Michael Seely (the Atomic Blender) and host Chris Keefer walk through the financing history of Olkiluoto 3, Flamanville, Hinkley Point C and Sizewell C to show that every large Western nuclear project of the 21st century has failed to find a private actor willing to bear both construction risk and long-term offtake risk simultaneously, and argue that SMRs and hyperscaler PPAs have so far relocated rather than solved that problem.

themes.theme: Core framework: construction risk vs. offtake risk

insights.claim: Nuclear project financing reduces to managing two distinct risks that must both be resolved before capital will commit: construction risk (will it get built on time/budget) and offtake/revenue risk (will guaranteed revenue exist once it's built, given 60-100 year asset lives).
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: Use this two-risk lens to evaluate any new nuclear financing announcement: ask specifically who bears construction risk and who bears offtake risk before assuming the deal is investable.
insights.quote: There are really like two large categories of risk. The first is the construction of the plant itself... And then the second part is the actual revenues that the plant will generate into the future.

insights.claim: Comparing nuclear power economics to wind, solar or gas turbines is a category error; the more accurate analogy is a hydroelectric dam, because both involve very long construction timelines, decades-long payback periods, and financing structures built around multi-decade revenue certainty.
insights.speaker: Chris Keefer
insights.so_what: Investors benchmarking nuclear developer economics against renewables or gas peakers are using the wrong comparable; hydro/infrastructure financing multiples are the better reference class.

insights.claim: Combined-cycle gas turbine plants can be built in about two years with low, well-known construction risk and hundreds are built globally per year, often under simple fixed-price contracts, making them a structurally cheaper-to-finance baseline that nuclear must compete against.
insights.speaker: Chris Keefer
insights.so_what: Gas's short-cycle, low-risk financing profile is the real competitive benchmark that any new nuclear financing structure has to beat, not just LCOE.

insights.claim: The cost of capital embedded in financing charges can be roughly 60% of a nuclear plant's final levelized cost of electricity on a high-risk project like Hinkley Point C, versus roughly 20-25% for older, well-managed, sovereign-debt-financed European nuclear builds from the 1980s.
insights.speaker: Chris Keefer
insights.so_what: Financing cost, not construction cost per se, is the dominant driver of nuclear LCOE, which means de-risking capital structure matters more than incremental engineering cost cuts.
insights.quote: The cost of capital essentially for that project is going to end up being something like 60% um, of the the final, you know, levelized cost of electricity out of that plant.

insights.claim: A roughly 3-percentage-point rise in prevailing interest rates (from about 2-3% to about 6% in the US/Canada) shrinks the size of project a given amount of capital can support by roughly 30-40%, illustrating why multi-decade, capital-intensive nuclear builds are uniquely rate-sensitive.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: Nuclear project economics should be modeled as highly duration-sensitive fixed-income-like assets; rate cycles matter as much as engineering execution to project viability.

themes.theme: Olkiluoto 3 (Finland): the consortium model that bankrupted Areva

insights.claim: Finland's Olkiluoto 3 EPR used a consortium of roughly 30 Nordic industrial companies and utilities that pre-committed to buy power at a fixed price for decades to guarantee project revenue, while EDF/Areva took on 100% of construction risk under a turnkey fixed-price contract.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: This consortium-offtake model is a template worth tracking for future large reactors where a captive industrial buyer base exists, even though it left the builder catastrophically exposed.

insights.claim: The Olkiluoto 3 EPR design was not actually finished when Areva signed the fixed-price construction contract, which drove years of design changes, quality-control problems, and instrumentation-and-control cabling that had to be rewired three times, pushing the project roughly a decade past its planned completion.
insights.speaker: Chris Keefer / Michael Seely
insights.so_what: First-of-a-kind reactor designs signed under fixed-price turnkey contracts before licensing/design finalization is a specific red flag pattern investors should screen for in new-build announcements.
insights.quote: The design of the EPR isn't really finished when the contract is signed.

insights.claim: Areva's losses on Olkiluoto 3 were severe enough that the French government had to bail it out, carving the money-losing construction unit into a separate debt vehicle, merging the surviving business into what became Framatome, and spinning off the fuel-supply business as Orano.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: State-owned reactor vendors can effectively be broken up by cost overruns on a single project; corporate structure of EDF/Framatome today is a direct legacy of this restructuring.

insights.claim: The Olkiluoto 3 offtake consortium sued Areva/EDF over the roughly decade-long delay and settled for a payout in the hundreds of millions of dollars.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: Delay litigation risk is a real, quantifiable cost line in turnkey nuclear contracts and should be underwritten explicitly in any new fixed-price nuclear deal.

insights.claim: Despite the Olkiluoto 3 delay, the Finnish offtake consortium was largely insulated from the cost overrun because the risk was contractually placed on Areva/EDF and ultimately absorbed by French taxpayers rather than Finnish electricity consumers.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely / Chris Keefer
insights.so_what: Contract structure, not project performance, determines who eats overrun losses; the same physical failure can be a non-event for one country's ratepayers and a fiscal crisis for another's taxpayers.

themes.theme: Flamanville: home-turf familiarity didn't prevent a second disaster

insights.claim: EDF's Flamanville 3 EPR, built in France by EDF itself with an already-familiar regulator and supply chain, still took roughly 20 years from the mid-2000s start to grid connection, with the French national auditor later finding EDF had underestimated both design-finalization difficulty and the difficulty of meeting regulatory quality requirements.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: Familiarity with the regulator and domestic supply chain did not meaningfully de-risk execution, undercutting the common assumption that 'building at home' de-risks nuclear construction.

insights.claim: Because both the customer (EDF, the utility) and the builder (Areva/EDF) at Flamanville were state-owned or state-adjacent entities, the ultimate financial backstop for the overrun was always the French state, either through higher electricity prices or additional sovereign borrowing.
insights.speaker: Chris Keefer
insights.so_what: Vertically state-integrated nuclear programs (buyer and builder both state-owned) effectively socialize all project risk onto the sovereign balance sheet regardless of contract structure.

themes.theme: Hinkley Point C: Contract for Difference shifts price risk, not construction risk

insights.claim: Hinkley Point C was financed by EDF and China General Nuclear (CGN) under a UK Contract for Difference with a strike price of roughly £90/megawatt-hour indexed to inflation — about 2.5-3x the average UK market electricity price when it was set — designed to embed a construction-cost margin into the long-term power price rather than to reflect prevailing market rates.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely / Chris Keefer
insights.so_what: A strike price multiple of 2.5-3x prevailing market power prices is a useful benchmark for evaluating whether other proposed nuclear CfDs are pricing in adequate construction-risk margin.

insights.claim: Because Hinkley Point C's Contract for Difference only pays out once electricity is actually generated, a construction delay of 10-15 years means interest and cost overruns keep accruing with zero offsetting revenue, which is the mechanism that undermined the model's original goal of protecting the project from cost blowouts.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: Revenue-on-completion contract structures (CfDs, PPAs) do not actually hedge construction risk for the developer — only revenue-during-construction structures like a Regulated Asset Base do.

insights.claim: China General Nuclear scaled back further project financing commitments at Hinkley Point C partly because UK government concerns about Chinese involvement and national sovereignty made the deal politically sensitive.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: Geopolitical/national-security scrutiny of foreign state capital is itself a project financing risk factor for nuclear builds with non-domestic equity partners.

insights.claim: Hinkley Point C's strike price was designed to deliver an internal rate of return of roughly 10-12%, but as construction delays stack up that embedded IRR erodes toward 3-4%, at which point the deal becomes less attractive than simply buying a risk-free 30-year government bond at a comparable yield.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: Track realized vs. embedded IRR on nuclear CfD projects as a leading indicator of investor appetite for follow-on nuclear deals — once IRR compresses near sovereign-bond yields, private capital exits.
insights.quote: Once you start getting down into the like four, five, six or 3% internal rate of return, that's really not attractive... they can go buy a 30-year bond from the US Treasury or from the this French treasury at that same rate and that is guaranteed 100%.

themes.theme: Sizewell C: Regulated Asset Base pushes construction risk onto ratepayers

insights.claim: Sizewell C uses a UK Regulated Asset Base (RAB) structure that lets the project start charging ratepayers during construction rather than only after commissioning, guaranteeing investors roughly a 10-12% return that begins accruing as soon as construction starts — a structural first among the European nuclear projects discussed.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: RAB is a distinct, replicable financing template (already used in UK water/energy infrastructure) that materially de-risks construction financing versus CfD or turnkey models — worth tracking for future reactor announcements outside the UK.

insights.claim: The UK government holds roughly 45-50% ownership of Sizewell C through a sovereign fund, with the remainder held by other investors including a UK government-linked infrastructure investment fund, a Quebec pension fund, and private infrastructure investors such as Centrica and Amber, none of whom is a reactor vendor or constructor.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: Sizewell C is the clearest current example of pension-fund-style infrastructure capital taking a real equity stake in nuclear construction risk, a pattern to watch for replication elsewhere.

insights.claim: The Regulated Asset Base model explicitly shifts construction risk (and some revenue risk) from the developer onto consumers, who begin prepaying for the plant before it exists — directly analogous to the construction-work-in-progress ratepayer charges used at Vogtle and VC Summer in the US.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: RAB and CWIP-style ratepayer prepayment are functionally the same risk transfer mechanism across the UK and US; regulatory approval of ratepayer prepayment is the real gating factor for new nuclear finance, not technology choice.

insights.claim: Michael Seely argues that bringing in private equity partners on projects like Sizewell C adds a genuine additional oversight/audit layer beyond what government-only financing provides, because large pension-fund investors actively scrutinize contract terms and construction decisions to protect their 80-year return horizon, pushing back on the view that private capital participation is purely symbolic.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: Presence of sophisticated long-horizon private investors (pension funds) in a nuclear cap table can be read as a signal of genuine project discipline, not just cosmetic risk-sharing.

themes.theme: US turnkey collapse: Vogtle, VC Summer, and Westinghouse's exit from EPC risk

insights.claim: Westinghouse's fixed-price contracts at Vogtle and VC Summer (AP1000 projects) were fixed in headline number only, layered with extensive conditional clauses shifting regulatory-timing and cooperation risk onto the customer; Westinghouse still absorbed enough uncontracted construction risk that it went bankrupt, with parent company Toshiba paying billions of dollars to Southern Company.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: So-called 'fixed-price' nuclear EPC contracts should be read closely for embedded conditional risk-shifting clauses rather than taken at face value from the headline contract price.

insights.claim: VC Summer ratepayers in South Carolina funded roughly $9 billion toward the project before it was abandoned with no completed plant delivered, followed by litigation over responsibility.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: VC Summer is the clearest cautionary precedent for regulators and investors evaluating any new US ratepayer-funded (CWIP-style) nuclear project: $9B in prepayment produced zero delivered generation.

insights.claim: Following the Vogtle and VC Summer losses, Western reactor vendors including Westinghouse and EDF/Framatome have explicitly exited the turnkey EPC business and now position themselves purely as technology licensors, deliberately avoiding construction-risk-bearing roles going forward.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: Any new large reactor deal that has a Western vendor bearing full EPC/construction risk again would be a striking reversal of stated industry strategy and worth flagging as either unusually favorable terms or a red flag.
insights.quote: Westinghouse... is very publicly stated. They're out of the turnkey EPC business now. They're a technology provider that will rely on the bectels of the world or somebody else in order to be able to actually get the project across the finish line.

themes.theme: SMRs and hyperscaler PPAs: the financing problem relocated, not solved

insights.claim: Michael Seely characterizes the pivot to small modular and micro reactors as fundamentally a response to unresolved financing risk rather than a technology-driven improvement, citing a peer's framing that SMRs are 'a nuclear engineering solution to a financing problem.'
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: Investors should evaluate SMR economics on financing-risk-reduction merits (faster build, more merchant-financeable) rather than assuming per-kW cost will beat large reactors — the underlying thesis is about deal structure, not physics.
insights.quote: James Crown called this a nuclear engineering solution to a financing problem.

insights.claim: Ontario Power Generation's Darlington SMR project, financed almost entirely through OPG's public rate base with only recent minority equity from government-linked funds, already has a first-unit cost of roughly US$4.5 billion, undercutting the SMR industry narrative that modularity would deliver dramatically lower absolute costs.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: A ~$4.5B first-of-a-kind SMR cost is a real-world data point to weigh against vendor cost claims when modeling SMR economics for any equity or project-finance analysis.

insights.claim: NuScale's SMR supply deal with the Utah Associated Municipal Power Systems (UAMPS) consortium collapsed specifically due to a lack of adequate financing, illustrating that offtake risk kills SMR projects even when a customer consortium has been assembled.
insights.speaker: Chris Keefer
insights.so_what: The NuScale/UAMPS failure is a precedent for assessing risk in any new SMR consortium-offtake deal; assembling buyers is not sufficient without a financing structure that survives cost escalation.

insights.claim: Hyperscalers including Microsoft, Amazon and Google are signing largely non-binding power purchase agreements contingent on a reactor actually being built and licensed, which effectively places close to 100% of construction and licensing risk on the reactor developer rather than sharing it, unlike the Vogtle/VC Summer model where ratepayers bore some risk.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: Headlines about hyperscaler nuclear PPAs should be read skeptically: non-binding, contingent offtake does not resolve developers' construction-financing gap and shouldn't be treated as equivalent to a firm revenue guarantee when sizing project risk.
insights.quote: The customer is taking on zero risk. They're just saying if you deliver it we'll buy it from you.

insights.claim: Amazon's funding of hundreds of millions of dollars to X-Energy is earmarked for technology development, not construction risk-bearing, making it a capped, low-risk capital commitment rather than a genuine offtake guarantee.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: Distinguish 'technology development' capital from true construction/offtake risk capital when assessing how derisked a given hyperscaler-backed reactor developer actually is.

insights.claim: Fermi America's proposed AP1000-plus-natural-gas-powered AI data center and industrial campus is struggling to sign firm offtake agreements for the power despite an aggressive push, showing that even highly motivated AI/data-center demand has not yet translated into binding long-term contracts needed to underwrite nuclear construction finance.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: The AI power-demand narrative driving nuclear equity interest should be checked against whether developers are actually landing signed, binding offtake — Fermi America's difficulty signing customers is a live counter-example to assume-the-demand-solves-financing narratives.

insights.claim: As of this conversation, only two SMR designs have concrete financial commitments behind them beyond marketing — TerraPower's Natrium plant (roughly 50% cost-sharing with the US Department of Energy) and X-Energy's Hermes demonstration plant — while every other announced SMR design remains without firm build-and-operate agreements.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: Use signed government cost-share or demonstration contracts, not press releases, as the filter for which SMR developers have moved past the 'PowerPoint' stage.

insights.claim: KEPCO and KHNP took an 18% equity stake in the Barakah nuclear project in the UAE alongside their role as builder/exporter, deliberately aligning their financial incentives with the plant's long-term operating performance rather than just its construction handover.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: Vendor equity co-investment (skin in the game) is a specific, checkable deal term that signals stronger long-term alignment than a pure fixed-price EPC contract with no ongoing stake.

themes.theme: Path forward: government as lender of last resort

insights.claim: Michael Seely's own conclusion is that government must act as lender of last resort for large nuclear projects, because in the current environment no private actor — vendors, EPCs, or owners — is willing to underwrite both construction risk and long-term offtake risk simultaneously despite generous US incentives like investment and production tax credits under the Inflation Reduction Act.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: Bet on nuclear new-build activity tracking government backstop policy (loan guarantees, DOE cost-share, sovereign equity) more closely than tax-credit generosity alone, since credits already exist and haven't unlocked private financing on their own.
insights.quote: I think it has to be government as the sort of lender of last resort here because no one else is is willing to do it in the current environment.

insights.claim: The Department of Energy's guaranteed offtake for Centrus's Halo enrichment cascade is cited as a precedent for government-backed demand certainty, but Michael Seely argues this model likely doesn't scale to electricity because power is a highly commoditized good, and a government commitment to buy trillions of megawatt-hours over 80 years would be politically very difficult to sell.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: Don't extrapolate DOE's guaranteed-offtake precedent in enriched uranium/HALEU directly to expectations of a similar government electricity-purchase guarantee for reactors — the political economy differs materially.

insights.claim: Prior to the 1980s-90s, most Western nuclear plants (built by entities like EDF, Ontario Hydro, and Korea's state utilities, or by US regulated monopolies) were financed cheaply through sovereign or regulated-monopoly balance sheets; the later shift toward requiring private capital participation, associated with the neoliberal-era policy shift under Thatcher, raised the effective cost of capital for new nuclear.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely / Chris Keefer
insights.so_what: The historical baseline for 'cheap' nuclear power was sovereign-backed capital, not private markets; any policy push back toward direct sovereign/utility balance-sheet financing should be read as a bullish signal for new nuclear cost competitiveness.

insights.claim: The 1980s Washington Public Power Supply System ('Whoops') bankruptcy is cited as the historical counter-example disproving the assumption that a large captive ratepayer base automatically makes a regulated utility immune to default risk from nuclear cost overruns.
insights.speaker: Chris Keefer
insights.so_what: Even 'safe' regulated-utility nuclear financing structures have a documented default precedent (WPPSS); this tempers assumptions that utility rate-base financing is a risk-free path for new nuclear.

insights.claim: Michael Seely notes that Japan's first Gen 3+ plant, the ABWR at Kashiwazaki-Kariwa, was built in about 38 months, and this fast timeline gave Western vendors like Westinghouse and EDF false confidence in the early 2000s that the EPR and AP1000 could also be built in roughly 36 months, which encouraged them to offer risky turnkey fixed-price contracts that later blew out by years.
insights.speaker: Michael Seely
insights.so_what: The industry's own construction-timeline overconfidence, not just technical novelty, is a specific historical root cause of the fixed-price contract disasters at Olkiluoto and Vogtle — a useful diligence flag whenever a vendor cites a fast comparable build to justify pricing.
